content: Headache diagnosis uses ICHD-3 criteria and clinical evaluation. Tension-type headache: ≥10 episodes with ≥2 of: bilateral location, pressing/tightening quality, mild-moderate intensity, not aggravated by routine activity; no nausea/vomiting, ≤1 of photophobia/phonophobia. Cluster headache: ≥5 attacks with severe unilateral orbital/temporal pain lasting 15-180 minutes untreated, plus ≥1 ipsilateral autonomic symptom (conjunctival injection, lacrimation, nasal congestion, rhinorrhea, forehead/facial sweating, miosis, ptosis, eyelid edema) and/or sense of restlessness. Red flags requiring imaging/laboratory workup (SNOOP4): Systemic symptoms (fever, weight loss, night sweats), Neurologic deficit (focal signs, altered consciousness, papilledema), Onset sudden (thunderclap—peak <1 minute), Onset after age 50 (consider GCA—check ESR/CRP), Pattern change (progressive worsening), Positional headache (worse upright suggests CSF leak; worse supine suggests IIH), Precipitated by Valsalva, Pregnancy/postpartum (CVST, preeclampsia, RCVS), Painful eye with autonomic features (rule out acute angle-closure glaucoma), Post-traumatic onset. Diagnostic tests: ESR/CRP for GCA, LP for IIH (opening pressure >25 cm H2O) or meningitis, MRI/MRV for structural/vascular causes, CT for acute hemorrhage.
